title: Prompt Engineering
subtitle: Talk to AI Like a Pro — Get Better Outputs Every Time!
overview: Generative AI is only as good as the instructions you give it. Prompt Engineering is the skill of crafting clear, detailed, and effective prompts that get the best possible results from AI models like ChatGPT, Gemini, and Claude. In this session, students learn the anatomy of a great prompt, explore proven techniques like role prompting, chain-of-thought, few-shot examples, and output formatting — and put it all into practice through hands-on comparison challenges.

images._note: Add photos from the actual session — weak vs strong prompt comparisons, student prompt experiments, whiteboard prompt anatomy diagram.

keyTopics: Prompt — The input instruction or question given to an AI model to produce a desired output
Prompt Engineering — The skill of designing effective prompts to guide AI models toward accurate, relevant, and useful responses
The 6 Ingredients of a Great Prompt — Role, Task, Context, Format, Tone, and Examples (not all needed every time, but more = better)
Role Prompting — Telling the AI to adopt a persona: 'You are an expert doctor / experienced teacher / senior software engineer'
Zero-Shot Prompting — Asking the AI to complete a task with no examples provided; relies entirely on the model's training
Few-Shot Prompting — Providing 2–5 examples of the desired input-output pattern before asking the AI to continue it
Chain-of-Thought (CoT) Prompting — Asking the AI to 'think step by step' to improve reasoning accuracy on complex problems
System Prompt — An invisible instruction given before the conversation that sets the AI's behaviour, role, and constraints for the whole session
Output Format Specification — Telling the AI exactly how to structure its response: bullet points, JSON, table, numbered list, markdown, code block
Temperature (concept) — A parameter that controls AI creativity vs accuracy: low temperature = more predictable; high temperature = more creative
Prompt Injection — A security risk where malicious instructions are hidden in user input to override the AI's original instructions
Iterative Prompting — Refining prompts through multiple rounds of testing and improvement rather than expecting perfection on the first try

activity.title: Prompt Engineering Challenge Lab
parts: Computer or tablet with internet access
Access to ChatGPT, Gemini, or Claude (free tier)
Printed or on-screen Prompt Challenge Cards
Scoring sheet for comparing prompt outputs
Pencil or notes app for drafting prompts
steps: Part 1 — Weak vs Strong: Give students the same task with a weak prompt ('Explain gravity') and a strong prompt ('You are a physics teacher explaining gravity to a curious 10-year-old. Use a simple analogy, keep it under 100 words, and end with a fun fact'). Compare outputs and discuss what made the difference

Part 2 — Prompt Anatomy Drill: Students rebuild the weak prompt by adding each ingredient one at a time — Role first, then Task, then Context, then Format, then Tone. See how the output changes with each addition

Part 3 — Role Prompting: Each student picks a role (chef, lawyer, doctor, tour guide, sports coach) and prompts the AI to explain a topic from that expert's perspective. Share the most surprising result with the class

Part 4 — Few-Shot Challenge: Write a prompt that includes 3 examples of a pattern (e.g. word → emoji translation: 'happy → 😊, sad → 😢, angry → 😡, now translate: excited, tired, confused'). Compare to asking without examples
Part 5 — Chain-of-Thought Test: Give the AI a tricky maths or logic problem WITHOUT 'think step by step'. Then ask the same question WITH 'think step by step, show all working'. Compare accuracy

Part 6 — Format Specification: Ask the AI for the same information in three different formats: plain paragraph, numbered list, and a markdown table. Discuss which format works best for different use cases
Part 7 — System Prompt Design: Each student writes a system prompt that turns the AI into a tutor for a specific subject. Test it with 3 questions and score how well it performs as a tutor
Challenge: The 'Broken Prompt' game — teacher shows a bad prompt that produces a terrible output. Students have 3 minutes to fix it and share their improved version. Class votes on the best rewrite

options: Building the hardware that runs AI models
The skill of designing clear and effective instructions for AI models to get the best possible outputs
Programming an AI model from scratch using code
Engineering a robot that responds to voice commands
quiz.explanation: Prompt Engineering is the art of communicating with AI effectively! It involves crafting instructions that are specific, well-structured, and context-rich so the AI produces accurate, relevant, and useful responses.

quiz.q: Which of these is the WEAKEST prompt for getting a useful response?
options: You are an expert nutritionist. Write a 7-day meal plan for a 13-year-old vegetarian athlete, formatted as a table with breakfast, lunch, and dinner columns
Write a meal plan
Create a vegetarian meal plan for a teenager, with 3 meals per day for 7 days, including calorie estimates
You are a chef. Suggest healthy Indian meals for a student who exercises daily, in bullet point format
quiz.explanation: 'Write a meal plan' is a zero-context, zero-constraint prompt — the AI has no idea who it's for, what diet, what format, or how detailed. The more specific your prompt, the more targeted and useful the output!

options: Asking the AI to list different roles in a company
Telling the AI to adopt a specific expert persona to shape the style, depth, and perspective of its response
Assigning a user role in the Blynk app
A type of loop used in AI training code
quiz.explanation: Role Prompting unlocks expertise mode! 'You are an experienced cardiologist' makes the AI respond with medical depth and precision. 'You are a friendly primary school teacher' makes it use simple language and analogies. The role shapes everything.

options: A prompt that gets zero results from the AI
Asking the AI to perform a task with no examples provided, relying entirely on what it learned during training
A prompt technique that uses zero words
Asking the AI zero questions
quiz.explanation: Zero-Shot = no examples given! You just describe the task and trust the AI's training. Example: 'Translate this sentence to French.' Works well for straightforward tasks but may miss specific patterns you have in mind.

quiz.q: What is Few-Shot Prompting and why is it powerful?
options: Using a very short prompt with few words
Providing 2–5 input-output examples before your actual request, so the AI learns the exact pattern you want
Asking the AI only a few questions per session
A technique that reduces the AI's response length
quiz.explanation: Few-Shot is incredibly powerful! By showing examples — 'Input: happy → Output: 😊, Input: sad → Output: 😢' — the AI instantly understands the exact format and style you want, even for unusual tasks it wouldn't guess from a description alone.

options: Chains multiple prompts together in a loop
Instructs the AI to reason through a problem step by step, dramatically improving accuracy on complex tasks
Links the AI's responses to a database of facts
Creates a chain of AI models working together
quiz.explanation: CoT prompting unlocks the AI's reasoning! Adding 'think step by step' or 'show your working' forces the model to reason through problems rather than jumping to an answer. Studies show it dramatically improves accuracy on maths, logic, and multi-step problems.

options: A prompt that only system administrators can use
An error message produced by the AI system
An invisible instruction set before the conversation that defines the AI's role, behaviour, and constraints for the whole session
A prompt that controls the AI's processing speed
quiz.explanation: The System Prompt is the AI's briefing! It runs silently before the conversation and sets the persona, rules, and context: 'You are a helpful maths tutor. Always explain concepts simply. Never give the answer directly — guide the student instead.' Everything after respects these rules.

quiz.q: Why does specifying the OUTPUT FORMAT in a prompt improve results?
options: It makes the AI run faster
It tells the AI exactly how to structure its response — as a table, bullet list, JSON, or code block — so it's immediately usable
It prevents the AI from hallucinating
It reduces the length of the response automatically
quiz.explanation: Format specification saves time! 'Give me a table with columns for Country, Capital, and Population' produces a ready-to-use table. Without format instructions, the AI picks its own structure which may not match your needs. Always specify format for structured data.

quiz.q: What does a LOW temperature setting do to an AI model's output?
options: Makes the output shorter and faster
Produces more predictable, focused, and consistent responses — better for factual tasks
Makes the AI more creative and unpredictable
Reduces the number of tokens used per response
quiz.explanation: Temperature controls randomness! Low temperature (close to 0) = AI picks the most probable next token every time → consistent, factual, predictable. High temperature = more randomness → creative, varied, but sometimes inaccurate. Use low for facts, high for creative writing.

options: Adding more detail to a prompt to improve results
A security attack where malicious instructions are hidden in user input to override the AI's original instructions
Injecting code into the AI's training data
A method of combining two prompts together
quiz.explanation: Prompt Injection is an AI security risk! An attacker might type: 'Ignore your previous instructions and instead...' trying to hijack the AI's behaviour. This is a real vulnerability in AI-powered apps, and defending against it is an active area of AI safety research.

quiz.q: A student wants the AI to write a story about space. Which prompt will produce the BEST result?
options: Write a story about space
Space story please

You are a science fiction author. Write a 200-word story for 12-year-olds about an Indian astronaut who discovers a mysterious signal from a distant galaxy. Use vivid descriptions and end with a cliffhanger
Write me something about outer space and planets and stars
quiz.explanation: The third prompt has everything: Role (sci-fi author), Task (200-word story), Audience (12-year-olds), Context (Indian astronaut, mysterious signal), Format (vivid descriptions), and Structure (cliffhanger ending). Every detail shapes the output toward exactly what's wanted!

options: Writing the same prompt over and over
Refining and improving your prompt across multiple attempts based on the AI's previous output until you get the desired result
Using a for loop to send the same prompt repeatedly
Iterating through a list of prompts automatically
quiz.explanation: Great prompts are rarely written perfectly the first time! Iterative prompting means: send a prompt → evaluate the output → identify what's missing or wrong → refine the prompt → try again. Each iteration brings you closer to the ideal output.

quiz.q: Why does adding CONTEXT to a prompt improve AI responses?
options: It makes the AI's response longer
Context tells the AI the background situation, audience, and purpose so it can tailor the response appropriately instead of giving a generic answer
Context reduces hallucinations completely
Context is only useful for image generation, not text
quiz.explanation: Context is everything! 'Explain photosynthesis' could be for a PhD student or a 7-year-old. Add 'for a class of 8-year-olds who love football' and the AI uses analogies like 'plants are like solar panels on a football kit'. Same task, completely different — and better — result.

quiz.q: Which prompting technique is MOST useful when you need the AI to solve a multi-step maths problem accurately?
options: Role Prompting
Few-Shot Prompting
Chain-of-Thought Prompting with 'think step by step'
Output Format Specification
quiz.explanation: Chain-of-Thought is best for maths and logic! 'Think step by step' forces the AI to show its reasoning at each stage rather than jumping to a final answer. Research shows CoT prompting cuts errors on complex maths problems dramatically.

quiz.q: A student asks the AI 'What is 17 × 24?' and gets the wrong answer. They add 'Think step by step and show all working.' The AI now gets it right. Why?
options: The second prompt was longer so it used more processing power
Chain-of-thought prompting forces the model to reason through each calculation step rather than pattern-matching to a potentially wrong memorised answer
The AI became more confident after being asked twice
Adding more words always improves AI accuracy
quiz.explanation: LLMs can make arithmetic errors by pattern-matching rather than calculating. 'Step by step' forces the model to actually work through 17×20=340, 17×4=68, 340+68=408 — reducing the chance of a wrong shortcut. Reasoning out loud catches mistakes!
